title: The Paris Commune
summary: For seventy-two days in the spring of 1871, working-class Paris threw out the conservative national government and ran the city itself. The Commune separated church and state, handed idle workshops to worker cooperatives, and flew the red flag. In late May the French army fought its way back in the Bloody Week, killing many thousands in combat and summary executions — older estimates range from 10,000 to 20,000; in its final days the Commune shot hostages and burned public buildings. Karl Marx hailed it as the first workers' government; to its enemies it was catastrophe. Both readings outlasted it.
hook: For seventy-two days in 1871, radical and working-class Paris governed itself. Then the state spent a week killing them for it. France had just lost a humiliating war to Prussia, and the people of Paris, hungry from a long siege and abandoned by a conservative government that fled to Versailles, seized the capital and governed it themselves. They called it the Commune. It separated church from state, decreed free and secular schooling, gave shuttered workshops to the workers, and raised the red flag over the city hall. It was idealistic, chaotic, and short. In late May the French army fought its way back street by street and carried out mass executions, killing many thousands of Parisians — older estimates often run from ten to twenty thousand — in what came to be called the Bloody Week. In its last days the Commune too shot hostages, among them the Archbishop of Paris, and set parts of the city ablaze. To Karl Marx it was the first government of the working class and a martyrdom; to its enemies, a violent and doomed insurrection. It was both buried and immortal.

beginner.theEvent:
In the spring of 1871, the people of Paris took control of their own city. For about two months, workers and ordinary citizens ran the capital of France. They called it the Commune. They raised a red banner above the town hall instead of the national flag. They tried to build a fairer city, run by the people who lived in it.

It did not last. In late May, the French national army marched in to take the city back. The fighting was bloody. When it was over, soldiers shot thousands of Parisians, many of them after they had already surrendered. The week of killing became known as the Bloody Week. The Commune was crushed, but its memory never died.
beginner.context:
To understand the Commune, you have to start with a lost war. In 1870, France went to war with Prussia, a powerful German state. France lost badly. The emperor was captured, and his government fell. Prussian armies then surrounded Paris and laid siege to it.

The siege lasted months. People in Paris grew hungry. Some ate rats. Through it all, the citizens kept their own armed force, the National Guard, made up of ordinary Parisians. When France finally gave up and signed a hated peace, many in Paris felt betrayed. A new conservative government took power and based itself in Versailles, outside the city. It did not trust radical, working-class Paris. Paris did not trust it either.
beginner.theRecord:
Much of what we know comes from the records of the time. There were newspapers, posters, and the decrees the Commune itself published. There were army reports, trial records, and the letters and memoirs of people who lived through it. Photographs survive too, including pictures of the ruins after the fighting.

The records show how it began. In March 1871, the Versailles government sent soldiers to take away cannons that the Paris National Guard had kept. The cannons sat on the hills of the city. The plan failed. Soldiers refused to fire on the crowd. Paris rose up. The government fled to Versailles. The city held elections and chose its own council, the Commune, to govern itself.
beginner.phases:
The Commune came in stages. First came the rising. In March 1871, Paris refused to give up its cannons and threw out the national government. The city elected its Commune and took charge of its own affairs.

Then came the experiment. Through April and into May, the Commune governed Paris. It passed laws, ran schools, and tried to reshape daily life. At the same time, the army at Versailles built up its strength and shelled the city from outside.

Then came the end. In late May the army broke through the walls and fought its way in, street by street. This was the Bloody Week. The soldiers killed in huge numbers. The Commune, in its final days, killed hostages and burned buildings. By the end of May, it was over.
beginner.mechanics:
The Commune worked through ordinary people taking power. The men who ran it were workers, shopkeepers, teachers, and writers, not the usual rich and powerful. They wanted the city run by and for the people who labored in it.

The Commune made bold changes fast. It separated the church from the government. It made schooling free and took it out of the hands of the church. It gave workshops that owners had abandoned to groups of workers to run together. It said officials should not be paid more than a skilled worker earned. These were huge ideas for the time.

But the Commune was also divided and rushed. Its leaders argued among themselves. They had little time and an army at their gates. Much was promised, and much was left unfinished.
beginner.transformation:
The Bloody Week changed everything. When the army retook Paris, it did not just defeat the Commune. It punished the city. Soldiers lined people up against walls and shot them. Many had surrendered. Some were chosen simply for looking poor or for having dirty hands, as if that proved they had fought.

No one knows the exact number killed. Careful estimates put the dead at ten thousand or more. It was the bloodiest week the city had seen in a century. The Commune, in its last days, also did terrible things. It shot hostages it held, including the Archbishop of Paris, the city's most senior priest. It set fire to parts of the city. Both sides spilled blood, but the army's killing was far larger.
beginner.causation:
Why did Paris rise, and why was it crushed so hard? Several reasons came together.

The lost war was the spark. The defeat by Prussia and the long siege left Paris angry, armed, and feeling betrayed by its leaders.

Class anger ran deep. Paris was full of workers who had little and wanted a fairer share. They distrusted the rich men now running France from Versailles.

Fear drove the violence. The government and the wealthy were terrified of a workers' revolt. They saw the red flag and thought of chaos and the end of property. That fear helped turn the army's victory into a massacre.

The two sides could not talk. Each saw the other as an enemy of France itself.
beginner.significance:
The Commune lasted only about ten weeks. Yet it became one of the most famous events in the history of the left. For Karl Marx, the thinker behind modern communism, it was the first time the working class had ever truly held power. He called it a glimpse of a new kind of society. Later revolutionaries, like Lenin in Russia, studied it closely.

To others, the Commune was a warning. They saw it as proof that revolution brought only violence and ruin. Both sides remembered it, and both used it.

In Paris, the dead were not forgotten. A wall in a famous cemetery, where the last fighters were shot, became a place of memory. Each year, people still gather there. The Commune lives on as both a dream and a grave.

general.theEvent:
Picture Paris in the spring of 1871. A red flag hangs over the Hôtel de Ville. The city has just lived through a brutal siege and a national defeat, and now it has thrown out the government of France and is governing itself. Workers debate decrees in the city hall. The National Guard, a citizen army of ordinary Parisians, holds the streets. For about seventy-two days, the capital belongs to its own people, who call their experiment the Commune.

The Paris Commune of 1871 was a brief, radical, self-governing rising that ended in slaughter. After France lost the Franco-Prussian War, the people of Paris refused the peace and the conservative government that signed it, seized the city, and tried to build a democratic, working-class order. For two months they reorganized civic life on egalitarian lines. Then the national army retook the city in a week of street fighting and mass executions. What makes the Commune matter is not its length but what it attempted and how it died, and the enormous symbolic weight both gave it.
general.context:
The Commune is unintelligible without the catastrophe of 1870 and 1871. France, under the Second Empire of Napoleon III, blundered into war with Prussia in 1870 and was crushed. The emperor was captured at Sedan, his regime collapsed, and a new republic fought on. Prussian forces besieged Paris through the autumn and winter. The city starved, bombarded and cut off, while its citizens kept arms through the National Guard.

When France finally surrendered in early 1871, the humiliation was total: lost provinces, a huge indemnity, and a German empire proclaimed at Versailles. A conservative National Assembly, elected by a largely rural and monarchist-leaning France, took power under Adolphe Thiers and sat at Versailles rather than in radical Paris. The capital, armed and embittered, regarded this government as ready to betray both the republic and the city. The mutual distrust between working-class Paris and the conservative provinces set the stage for rupture.
general.theRecord:
The record of the Commune is dense and partisan. The Commune published its own decrees, proclamations, and newspapers; the Versailles side produced army dispatches, the records of military tribunals, and a hostile press. Memoirs poured out from both camps, and early photography captured the barricades and the burned shells of public buildings. Because nearly everyone involved had a cause to defend, the sources must be read against the grain.

The rupture itself is well documented. On 18 March 1871, Thiers ordered the army to seize cannons that the National Guard held on the heights of Montmartre and Belleville. The operation failed; soldiers fraternized with the crowd, and two generals were seized and shot. The government withdrew to Versailles. Paris held municipal elections on 26 March and proclaimed the Commune two days later. What followed was effectively a civil war between two French governments, one in the capital and one at Versailles, lasting until the army's reconquest at the end of May.
general.phases:
The Commune falls into clear phases. The first was the seizure of the city in mid-March 1871, triggered by the failed attempt to disarm the National Guard, followed by the flight of the government and the election of the Commune's council.

The second was the brief period of self-government through April and most of May. The Commune issued a stream of decrees while internal factions argued over strategy and authority. A Committee of Public Safety, named after a body from the French Revolution, was created late in the period as the military situation worsened, deepening divisions between moderates and radicals.

The third phase was the war itself. The Versailles army, rebuilt with prisoners released by Prussia, besieged and shelled Paris through April and May. The final phase was the Bloody Week, the Semaine sanglante, beginning 21 May. The army entered the city and fought from west to east across a week of barricades, fires, and mass killing. It ended in the working-class districts around Père Lachaise on 28 May.
general.mechanics:
The Commune's radicalism lay in concrete measures, and naming who enacted them matters. Its council, dominated by workers, artisans, journalists, and a minority of professional revolutionaries, governed through commissions. It separated church and state and ended religious teaching, making primary education free, secular, and open to girls. It transferred workshops abandoned by owners who had fled to cooperatives of their employees. It abolished night work in bakeries, suspended rent and debt obligations accumulated during the siege, and capped the salaries of its own officials at the wage of a skilled worker. The red flag replaced the tricolor as the city's emblem.

The Commune's weakness lay in the same place as its character. It was a coalition, not a party: Blanquists who wanted centralized revolutionary authority, Proudhonian socialists who favored federation and cooperation, neo-Jacobins who looked back to 1793, and unaligned radicals all pulled against one another. It never marched on Versailles in the early days when it might have prevailed, and it spent precious time in internal dispute. Figures like the teacher and activist Louise Michel became emblems of its energy, but no single hand directed it.
general.transformation:
The Bloody Week transformed the Commune from a political experiment into a massacre and a martyrdom. When the army broke into Paris on 21 May, it fought through a week of barricades. As it advanced, it carried out summary executions on a vast scale, shooting captured fighters and suspected sympathizers, sometimes on no more evidence than powder-stained hands or a poor man's clothing. Prisoners were marched out and shot in groups against walls and in public squares.

The death toll is genuinely uncertain and has been revised by scholars. Long-standing estimates run from roughly 10,000 to as high as 20,000 or more Parisians killed. Some recent work argues for a figure nearer the lower end. By any count, it was the bloodiest week in nineteenth-century Paris. The Commune's own violence belongs in the record alongside the repression. In its final days it executed hostages it had seized, among them Georges Darboy, the Archbishop of Paris, and it burned public buildings, including the Tuileries Palace and the Hôtel de Ville. The scale of the two killings was not equal, but both were real.
general.causation:
No single cause produced the Commune; it was a convergence. The lost war supplied the detonator. The defeat by Prussia, the siege, and the despised peace left Paris armed, hungry, and convinced its leaders had failed the nation. The National Guard gave the city the means to resist, and Thiers's attempt to disarm it gave the immediate trigger.

Deeper structural forces lay beneath. Paris was a city of workers and artisans shaped by decades of industrial growth and urban transformation, with a long memory of insurrection reaching back through 1848 and the great revolution of 1789. Class division between this laboring capital and the propertied, rural-dominated assembly at Versailles ran through everything.

The ferocity of the suppression had its own causes. The Versailles government and the propertied classes were seized by fear of social revolution, reading the red flag as the threat of property's abolition and of chaos spreading from the capital. That fear, joined to the army's humiliation in the recent war, helped turn military reconquest into mass execution.
general.significance:
The Paris Commune endures less for what it achieved than for what it came to mean. It governed for barely ten weeks and left few lasting institutions, yet it became one of the most potent symbols in modern politics. On the available accounts, its significance is above all symbolic and contested, claimed and condemned by opposing traditions that still invoke it.

For the left, the Commune became sacred ground. Karl Marx, writing within weeks in 'The Civil War in France,' hailed it as the first time the workers had governed themselves. He read its forms as a glimpse of how a workers' state might rule. Lenin later treated it as a practical model and a lesson in revolution. Communist and socialist movements made its memory a rite, gathering at the Communards' Wall in Père Lachaise where the last fighters were shot. For conservatives and many liberals, the Commune was a warning: proof that revolution unleashed violence, destroyed property, and ended in ruin.

These readings have hardened into rival legends, and serious accounts caution against both the heroic and the demonic versions. The Commune was idealistic and disorganized, creative and lethal, crushed with a brutality out of all proportion to its threat. That it could be remembered as both a martyrdom of socialism and a cautionary tale of disorder is the surest sign of how much weight a brief rising came to carry.

advanced.theEvent:
The interpretive difficulty of the Commune is that it has been made to bear far more symbolic weight than its seventy-two days can naturally support. The scholarship has labored to recover the event from the legends built upon it. The dominant frame, set by Marx within weeks in 'The Civil War in France,' read the Commune as the first dictatorship of the proletariat and a model of the workers' state. Later writers from Lenin onward absorbed it into a teleology of proletarian revolution. Against this, social-historical work, associated above all with Jacques Rougerie, recovered the Commune as it was lived rather than as it was later theorized. It was a movement rooted in the artisanal and small-workshop world of Parisian labor, more republican and federalist than Marxist, improvising under siege.

This reframing carries an uncomfortable edge for both traditions. It denies the consoling clarity of the Marxist legend, in which a class-conscious proletariat enacted a coherent program, and equally the conservative legend of a nihilistic mob. The Communards were a heterogeneous coalition whose dominant strains owed more to Proudhon and to the memory of 1793 than to Marx, who had little direct influence on the events he immortalized. The Commune the participants made and the Commune the doctrine later required are not the same object.
advanced.context:
The Commune's relationship to the revolutionary tradition is genuinely contested ground. One reading locates it as the last of the nineteenth-century Parisian insurrections, the heir of 1789, 1830, and 1848, a final eruption of the journée and the barricade in their classic form. On this account the Commune looks backward, its neo-Jacobin Committee of Public Safety and its republican vocabulary marking it as the culmination of an old tradition rather than the opening of a new one.

A competing reading, the one that prevailed politically, treats the Commune as the first event of a new socialist era, the inaugural act of proletarian revolution. The difficulty is that both readings capture something real and neither is complete. The Commune's forms were largely inherited; its social composition and some of its measures pointed toward a labor politics that would outgrow the insurrectionary tradition. Locating the event requires holding these together. It was a rising that spoke the language of the revolutionary past while gesturing, in its cooperatives and its assault on clerical authority, toward a future its participants could not have named.
advanced.theRecord:
The evidentiary core of the Commune is secure on its main outlines but unstable at exactly the point where it matters most: the scale of the killing. That the army carried out mass summary executions during the Semaine sanglante is established beyond dispute; what the record cannot fix is the number. The high figures of 20,000 to 35,000, derived from contemporary estimates and long canonical, have been challenged by Robert Tombs. His work on burial records, military documents, and demographic data argues for a substantially lower total, perhaps closer to 10,000, while insisting the repression was nonetheless a deliberate atrocity.

This dispute has real analytical weight and resists easy resolution. The bodies were buried hastily in mass graves, cremated, or removed; many victims were never identified; and both Communard and Versailles sources had incentives to inflate or minimize. The lower revised figures do not soften the moral character of the event, which lay in the deliberate, often arbitrary execution of prisoners. They do, however, unsettle a number that had become a fixed point of left memory. The honest position holds the certainty of the atrocity together with the genuine uncertainty of its magnitude, refusing to let either dissolve the other.
advanced.phases:
The clustering of the Commune's decisive moments reveals how contingent its course was. The most consequential choice came at the outset, in the days after 18 March, when the Central Committee of the National Guard held power and might have marched on a momentarily defenseless Versailles. Instead it deferred to legality, organized elections, and surrendered the initiative. By the time the Commune was proclaimed, Thiers had been given the breathing space to rebuild an army from prisoners returned by Prussia. The window for a military solution in the Commune's favor, if it ever existed, closed in those first weeks.

The temporal pattern also exposes the radicalization that siege produced. The Committee of Public Safety, created in early May with its deliberate echo of 1793, split the Commune. It set those who embraced revolutionary dictatorship against a minority who rejected it as a betrayal of the federalist and democratic promise. The hostage executions and the burning of the city belong to this final, desperate phase, when a movement facing annihilation reached for the instruments of terror. To read these acts without the context of the massacre already underway is to distort them; to excuse them by that context is to evade them.
advanced.mechanics:
The procedural and social mechanics of the Commune expose the decisive variable in its character: it was a federation of impulses, not a state with a sovereign will. Power was dispersed among the elected council, its commissions, the Central Committee of the National Guard, and the armed neighborhoods, with no settled hierarchy among them. This dispersal was partly principle, an expression of the federalist and anti-authoritarian strain that distrusted centralized power, and partly the simple chaos of improvisation under bombardment. The result was a body capable of issuing sweeping decrees but incapable of coordinated military command.

Locating agency precisely sharpens the analysis. The Commune's most enduring measures, the secular schools, the worker cooperatives, the salary cap, expressed the priorities of an artisanal labor movement rather than an industrial proletariat in the later sense. Paris in 1871 was a city of small workshops, not great factories. The Marxist reading that made the Commune the prototype of the proletarian state thus imposed a category its social base did not quite fit. The participants were building a democratic municipal republic of producers; the doctrine that canonized them was describing something else, and the gap between the two is where much of the interpretive difficulty lives.
advanced.transformation:
The transformation of the Commune into a foundational myth demands more precision than either celebratory or hostile accounts allow. Marx's intervention was decisive and, in a sense, creative. 'The Civil War in France' did not so much describe the Commune as theorize what it might have been. It extracted from a federalist, largely non-Marxist rising a model of the workers' state that the Communards themselves had not articulated. The text's influence ran forward through Lenin's 'The State and Revolution,' which read the Commune as proof that the proletariat must smash rather than inherit the existing state. The Commune thus entered the canon of revolutionary socialism less as an event than as a doctrine, its meaning fixed by readers it never knew.

The counter-memory requires equal care. The Commune's afterlife on the right and in liberal memory cast it as the archetype of revolutionary disorder, an image the hostage killings and the fires were made to confirm. Recent work, notably Kristin Ross's, has tried to recover a third register, the Communards' own imaginative horizon, their internationalism and their experiments in everyday democratic life, against both the Leninist appropriation and the conservative caricature. That three traditions of memory contend over ten weeks of 1871 is itself the measure of how unfinished the event remains.
advanced.causation:
Explaining the ferocity of the suppression is harder than explaining the rising, and the intuitive account of military necessity does not survive scrutiny. The Commune posed no serious military threat to the French state by late May; the army's victory was assured before the worst of the killing. The mass executions that followed were therefore not a battlefield necessity but a political act, and their explanation lies in the class fear that gripped the propertied classes and the officer corps. To Thiers's government and its supporters, the Commune represented the specter of social revolution, the abolition of property, and the contagion of disorder; the repression aimed to extinguish that specter by terror.

This sequence matters for how the whole episode is understood. Suppose the scale of the killing is explained chiefly by the determination to deliver a lesson to the working class rather than by any operational requirement. The Commune is then less a failed seizure of power than a demonstration of the violence a threatened social order will deploy in self-defense. The recent humiliation in the war sharpened the army's appetite for a victory it could win, and the long European fear of revolution, alive since 1789 and renewed in 1848, supplied the ideological charge. The Commune was crushed not merely because it lost a war but because of what it was taken to mean.
advanced.significance:
The Commune's enduring significance lies in its function as a contested origin point, the event to which competing political traditions trace lineage and from which they draw incompatible lessons. For the revolutionary socialist tradition it became the first workers' government and a tactical and moral inheritance, studied for what it attempted and mourned for how it died. For the conservative and liberal traditions it became the limiting case of revolution's costs, invoked to discredit the radical aspiration as such. That a single brief event sustains both readings is not a failure of analysis but a feature of the event. It was genuinely creative and genuinely destructive, and which face one sees depends on where one stands.

The comparison to later revolutions must be drawn with discipline. Reading the Commune straightforwardly as a rehearsal for 1917, the frame Lenin's appropriation encourages, risks a teleology the evidence does not license. The Communards were not Bolsheviks, and their movement's dominant strains were federalist and republican rather than vanguardist. Yet the continuity it exposes is genuine. The Commune demonstrated two things at once. The working class could seize and govern a great city, however briefly, and an established order would answer such a seizure with a violence its own laws did not constrain. Those two lessons, the possibility and the price, are the inheritance the Commune left to every movement that has claimed it.

foundation.theStory: The Paris Commune was a radical, self-governing rising in the spring of 1871, when the working people of Paris seized their city and ran it themselves for about seventy-two days. It grew out of national catastrophe: France had lost the Franco-Prussian War, Paris had endured a long siege, and a conservative government at Versailles tried to disarm the city's National Guard. Paris rose, elected its own council, and for two months separated church and state, made schooling free and secular, gave idle workshops to worker cooperatives, capped officials' pay, and flew the red flag. In late May the national army retook the city in the Bloody Week, fighting street to street and killing many thousands in combat and summary executions, the figures often given as 10,000 to 20,000 but debated; in its final days the Commune shot hostages, including the Archbishop of Paris, and burned parts of the city. Understanding the Commune means understanding how a brief experiment became the founding myth of revolutionary socialism.
foundation.theDebate: Several debates organize the study of the Commune. One concerns its nature: whether it was the last of the old Parisian insurrections in the line of 1789 and 1848, or the first event of a new socialist era, as Marx and later Lenin claimed. A second concerns the scale of the killing in the Bloody Week, where long-standing estimates of 20,000 or more have been challenged by lower figures nearer 10,000, without altering the deliberate, atrocious character of the repression. A third concerns memory and meaning: how a federalist, largely non-Marxist rising came to be canonized as the prototype of the proletarian state, and how the same event could be remembered as both a martyrdom and a warning.
foundation.whyItMatters: The Commune matters as the moment modern revolutionary socialism acquired a founding event, a real instance of the working class seizing and governing a great city. It illuminates the relationship between war and revolution, the politics of class fear, and the violence a threatened social order will deploy in self-defense. It matters too because it became a symbol fought over by rival traditions, claimed by Marx and Lenin as a model of proletarian power and condemned by conservatives as the proof of revolution's ruin, a contest of memory that shaped the politics of the century that followed.
questions: In the first days after the rising, the Commune chose elections and legality rather than marching on a defenseless Versailles. What does this choice suggest about the kind of movement it was, and about why it ultimately failed militarily?

The army's mass executions came after its military victory was already assured. Why might a victorious government choose to kill thousands of prisoners anyway, and what does that choice reveal about what the Commune was taken to mean?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Modern study of the Commune rests on several major interventions. Karl Marx's 'The Civil War in France,' written within weeks, fixed the event's revolutionary meaning and shaped a century of reading it as the first workers' government. Jacques Rougerie's social history recovered the Communards as they actually were, an artisanal and republican movement more indebted to Proudhon and to 1793 than to Marx. Robert Tombs has reexamined the demographic record of the repression and argued for a lower death toll than the canonical figures, while insisting on the atrocity's deliberate character. John Merriman has narrated the Bloody Week in detail, and Kristin Ross has recovered the Communards' own political imagination against later appropriations. These approaches are complementary more than rival, each illuminating a different layer of a heavily mythologized event.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence is abundant but partisan: the Commune's decrees and press, Versailles army records and military-tribunal proceedings, memoirs from both sides, and early photographs of the barricades and ruins. This base supports firm conclusions about the sequence of events, the Commune's measures, and the fact of mass summary execution. It also supports the conclusion that the repression was a political act rather than a military necessity, since the killing followed the army's assured victory. Its limits are real. The death toll cannot be fixed, because bodies were buried in mass graves, burned, or removed, and both sides had reasons to distort the count. The inner motives of participants, and the precise weight of any single faction's program, are likewise hard to recover from sources shaped by the urgency of civil war and the bitterness of its aftermath.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A defensible account must hold several things together. It must present the Bloody Week as a deliberate atrocity while honestly marking that its scale is contested, neither inflating the canonical high figures as settled fact nor letting a lower estimate soften the moral judgment. It must record the Commune's own violence, the hostage executions and the fires, as real, while keeping the gross asymmetry of the two killings in view. On the Commune's meaning, the strong position presents the Marxist canonization attributed and qualified: Marx and Lenin made the Commune the model of proletarian revolution, but the participants' federalist, republican, and artisanal world fits that model imperfectly, as Rougerie showed. The firmest ground is the sequence of events and the deliberate character of the repression; the interpretation should foreground that while marking what the evidence cannot settle.
questions: Robert Tombs argues for a lower death toll in the Bloody Week than the figures long central to left memory, while still calling the repression an atrocity. How should a historian weigh a revised number against the moral and political meaning a higher figure had acquired?

Marx theorized the Commune as the first proletarian state, yet its dominant strains were federalist and republican rather than Marxist. How does recognizing that gap change the way we read the Commune's place in the history of socialism?

research.topic: The sequence of the rising, the self-government, and the Bloody Week
research.content: The basic course of the Commune is established beyond serious dispute. The rising of 18 March 1871 followed the government's failed attempt to seize National Guard cannons on Montmartre; the government withdrew to Versailles; Paris held elections on 26 March and proclaimed the Commune on 28 March. For roughly seventy-two days the Commune governed the city, enacting the separation of church and state, free secular education, the transfer of abandoned workshops to worker cooperatives, a cap on officials' salaries, and the adoption of the red flag. The Versailles army, rebuilt with prisoners released by Prussia, entered Paris on 21 May and reconquered it across the Semaine sanglante, ending around 28 May. These facts are settled; the debated questions concern the scale of the killing and the event's meaning, not the sequence.

research.topic: The repression as a deliberate atrocity rather than a military necessity
research.content: Scholarship has established that the mass summary executions of the Bloody Week were a deliberate political act, not a battlefield necessity. By late May the army's victory was assured, yet it shot captured Communards and suspected sympathizers in large numbers, often arbitrarily and after surrender. The killing is best explained by the class fear that gripped the Versailles government and the propertied classes, who read the Commune as the threat of social revolution and the abolition of property, and by the army's appetite for a decisive victory after its humiliation by Prussia. The Commune's own violence in its final days, the execution of hostages including Archbishop Georges Darboy and the burning of public buildings, is also documented, though on a far smaller scale than the army's repression. This framing is broadly accepted; disagreement concerns emphasis and magnitude, not the deliberate character of the suppression.

research.topic: The exact death toll of the Bloody Week
research.content: The precise number killed during the Semaine sanglante cannot be recovered. What evidence would resolve it: a complete, reliable accounting of the dead, identified and counted. Why unavailable: many victims were buried hastily in mass graves, cremated, or removed; large numbers were never identified; and both Communard and Versailles sources had strong incentives to inflate or minimize the figure. What can be responsibly inferred: that the killing was on the scale of many thousands and constituted the bloodiest week in nineteenth-century Paris; long-standing estimates run from roughly 10,000 to 20,000 or higher, while Robert Tombs's reexamination of burial and demographic records argues for a figure nearer the lower end. What must remain genuinely uncertain is the exact total, which has become a contested point precisely because it carries moral and political weight for how the event is remembered.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: The Communards and the working people of Paris
comparativeNarrative.name: The View from the Barricades
comparativeNarrative.content: Seen from inside the Commune, the rising was not chaos but a chance to build a fairer city. The people who manned the barricades were workers, artisans, teachers, and their families, who had starved through the siege and felt betrayed by the men now ruling from Versailles. This vantage reveals what the hostile accounts hide. The Commune was an experiment in democratic, everyday life, run by people the old order had never let govern. They opened secular schools, handed idle workshops to cooperatives, and debated their decrees in the open. For them the red flag meant dignity, not destruction. This view also carries the memory of the end. When the army came, ordinary Parisians were shot against walls in their thousands, and the survivors carried that grief into the politics of the next generation.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: The Versailles government and the propertied classes
comparativeNarrative.name: The View from Versailles
comparativeNarrative.content: To Thiers's government and the property-owning classes who backed it, the Commune was not a civic experiment but a mortal danger. They had just lost a war and a capital, and now the city had raised the red flag, suspended debts, and seized property. To them this was the specter of social revolution, the threat that the disorder of the streets would spread across France and abolish ownership itself. Seeing the Commune from this side explains the ferocity of the suppression. The killing was not blind panic alone but a determination to deliver a lesson the working class would not forget. It also shows how real fear and real cruelty fused. Men who believed they were saving civilization ordered the summary execution of thousands, and persuaded themselves the slaughter was justified.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: Marx, Lenin, and the revolutionary tradition
comparativeNarrative.name: The View from the Revolutionary Left
comparativeNarrative.content: For the socialist and communist movements that came after, the Commune was less an event to be described than an inheritance to be claimed. Karl Marx, writing within weeks, called it the first government of the working class and read its forms as a glimpse of how a workers' state might rule. Lenin later studied it as both a model and a warning, drawing the lesson that a revolution must dismantle the old state rather than inherit it. From this vantage the Commune's brevity matters less than its example. It proved, to those who looked back on it, that the workers could seize and govern a great city. This reading also shaped memory itself, turning the Communards' Wall in Père Lachaise into a place of pilgrimage. Yet later scholars caution that this canonization fixed a meaning the federalist, republican Communards had not themselves chosen.

comparativeSummary: The same ten weeks look entirely different from each vantage, and holding them together keeps the history honest. From the barricades, the Commune was a hopeful experiment in self-rule, run by people the old order had shut out. Then it became a massacre. From Versailles, it was the threat of social revolution, a danger so grave that killing thousands could feel like saving the country. From the revolutionary left that came after, it was an inheritance. It was the first workers' government and a lesson for the next revolution, though that claiming fixed a meaning its participants had not chosen. No single view is enough. The Communards show what was attempted and what was lost. The Versailles side shows why the suppression was so fierce. The later left shows how a brief rising became a lasting myth. The fight is not about the facts of the sequence but about what those ten weeks were taken to mean.

rabbitHole.label: The French Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: The Commune drew on a Parisian revolutionary tradition that ran back through 1848 to 1789, borrowing the language of the republic and even reviving the name of the Committee of Public Safety from 1793; that founding revolution is the inheritance the Commune invoked rather than created.

rabbitHole.label: Socialism
rabbitHole.reason: The Commune became the founding myth and martyrdom of the modern socialist movement, the real-world instance to which socialists pointed as proof that the working class could govern; this entry defers to socialism for the full development of the movement the Commune inspired.

rabbitHole.label: Communism
rabbitHole.reason: Marx and later Lenin made the Commune the template of proletarian revolution, reading it as the first workers' government and a lesson in how to seize the state; the Marxist-Leninist tradition canonized the event and is essential to understanding its afterlife.

rabbitHole.label: Nationalism
rabbitHole.reason: The Commune was born from the national humiliation of the lost Franco-Prussian War and a despised peace, and the rage at military defeat shaped both the rising and the ferocity of its suppression; the clash of national and class loyalties runs through the whole event.

rabbitHole.label: The Industrial Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: The Commune was made by the workers and artisans of an industrializing Paris, and its measures, from worker cooperatives to the regulation of night labor, addressed the conditions industrial change had created; that working class is the social ground the Commune stood on.

rabbitHole.label: Napoleon
rabbitHole.reason: The Bonapartist Second Empire of Napoleon III, whose collapse in the Franco-Prussian War opened the path to the Commune, traced its legitimacy to the first Napoleon; the imperial order whose fall created the vacuum the Commune filled is part of the event's deep background.

rabbitHole.label: The Revolutions of 1848
rabbitHole.reason: The earlier wave of European revolutions in 1848, including the rising and bloody June Days in Paris, was the immediate ancestor of the Commune, supplying the memory of insurrection and class conflict that the Communards consciously inherited.

reference.author: Robert Tombs
reference.title: The Paris Commune 1871
reference.annotation: The standard modern analytical history in English and the source for this entry's account of the rising, the self-government, and the suppression. Tombs's reexamination of the demographic record underpins the entry's treatment of the contested death toll and his argument that the repression was a deliberate political act rather than a military necessity.

reference.author: John Merriman
reference.title: Massacre: The Life and Death of the Paris Commune
reference.annotation: The fullest narrative reconstruction of the Bloody Week and its human reality. It anchors the entry's account of the summary executions and the texture of the street fighting, conveying the scale and arbitrariness of the killing while keeping the Commune's own violence in view.

reference.author: Jacques Rougerie
reference.title: Paris libre 1871
reference.annotation: The decisive social history recovering the Communards as they actually were, an artisanal and republican movement more indebted to Proudhon and 1793 than to Marx. It grounds the entry's advanced argument that the Commune the participants made and the doctrine later built upon it are not the same object.

reference.author: Karl Marx
reference.title: The Civil War in France
reference.annotation: The intervention, written within weeks, that fixed the Commune's revolutionary meaning and made it the first government of the working class for a century of readers. Read as evidence of how the Commune was canonized by the left, not as a neutral account of the events it theorizes.

reference.author: Kristin Ross
reference.title: Communal Luxury: The Political Imaginary of the Paris Commune
reference.annotation: Recovers the Communards' own political imagination, their internationalism and their experiments in everyday democratic life, against both the Leninist appropriation and the conservative caricature. Supplies the entry's sense of the Commune as a horizon of possibility its participants glimpsed but could not name.

reference.author: Prosper-Olivier Lissagaray
reference.title: History of the Paris Commune of 1871
reference.annotation: The classic eyewitness account by a Communard who fought on the barricades and survived. Read as a partisan but invaluable record of how the rising and its destruction were experienced from inside, and of how the defeated built their own memory of the event.

commerce.title: Massacre: The Life and Death of the Paris Commune
commerce.author: John Merriman
commerce.note: If the Commune is new to you, start here. Merriman tells the whole story as a gripping narrative, from the rising to the slaughter of the Bloody Week, and assumes no background while never softening the horror of the ending.

commerce.title: The Paris Commune: A Brief History
commerce.author: Carolyn J. Eichner
commerce.note: For a reader who wants a short, reliable overview before going deeper, this concise account lays out what happened and why it mattered, with particular attention to the women who shaped the Commune and are often left out of the older histories.

commerce.title: Communal Luxury: The Political Imaginary of the Paris Commune
commerce.author: Kristin Ross
commerce.note: Read this once you want to understand what the Communards themselves hoped to build. Ross recovers their ideas and their internationalism, and is the best way to see the Commune as more than the legend either the left or the right later made of it.

commerce.title: History of the Paris Commune of 1871
commerce.author: Prosper-Olivier Lissagaray
commerce.note: Turn to this when you want the voice of someone who was there. Lissagaray fought on the barricades and survived; his account is partisan and vivid, the founding eyewitness history that shaped how the defeated remembered their own rising.

commerce.title: The Paris Commune 1871
commerce.author: Robert Tombs
commerce.note: The book for the reader ready to weigh the evidence carefully. Tombs gives the standard analytical history, reexamines the disputed death toll, and shows why the suppression was a deliberate political act rather than a necessity of war.

commerce.title: The Civil War in France
commerce.author: Karl Marx
commerce.note: Read this to see how the Commune became a myth. Marx wrote it within weeks of the massacre, and his reading of the rising as the first workers' government shaped a century of revolutionary thought, whatever the actual Communards intended.

popularCulture.title: The Commune as the founding myth of the left
popularCulture.description: On the left, the Paris Commune became a sacred memory, the first time workers ran a city themselves. Socialists and communists held it up as proof their dream could be real. Each May, people still gather at the wall in Père Lachaise where the last fighters were shot. The memory inspires, but it also smooths over how divided and improvised the real Commune was.

popularCulture.title: 'The Internationale' and the Commune
popularCulture.description: The most famous song of the workers' movement was written by Eugène Pottier. He was a member of the Commune, and he wrote it soon after it was crushed. Sung around the world for over a century, it carries the Commune's defiance into every later fight. Most who sing it do not know its roots in 1871, so the song outgrew the event that made it.

popularCulture.title: La Commune (Paris, 1871) — Peter Watkins (2000)
popularCulture.description: Watkins filmed the Commune as if TV news cameras had been there. Hundreds of ordinary people played the Communards. The long film, nearly six hours, catches the energy and the arguments of a city run by its own people. It is staged, not a record of fact, but it shows the feel of the rising better than a normal drama.

popularCulture.title: Louise Michel and the memory of the Communards
popularCulture.description: Louise Michel was a teacher who fought for the Commune and was sent away for it. She became a lasting symbol of the rising. Streets and schools in France bear her name. Her fame keeps alive the memory of the women of the Commune. But it can also turn a messy, shared event into the story of a few heroes.

popularCulture.title: 'The Bloody Week' as a name for state massacre
popularCulture.description: The Bloody Week, in French the Semaine sanglante, became a name for a government that turns its army loose on its own people. The phrase holds the memory of thousands shot against walls after they had given up. People still use it when a state crushes a revolt with far more force than any danger could justify. It is a warning about what power does when it is afraid.
